title: URL Shortener
prompt: Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly. Users should be able to submit a long URL and receive a short URL. When someone visits the short URL, they should be redirected to the original long URL.

functional: Shorten a long URL to a unique short URL
Redirect short URL to original long URL
Analytics: click count per short URL
nonFunctional: Short URLs should be as short as possible
Redirects should not break under load

keyQuestions: What is the expected read:write ratio?

keyCalculations: Storage per URL: ~500 bytes => 500GB for 1B URLs
Short URL length: base62, 7 chars = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion possibilities
Cache: 20% of URLs get 80% of traffic => cache top 20% = 100GB

expectedEntities: urls: id, short_code, long_url, user_id, created_at, expires_at
clicks: id, url_id, timestamp, ip, user_agent, referer

suggestedTopics: Key generation: hash vs counter-based, collision handling
Caching strategy: cache-aside, write-through, eviction policy
Database choice: SQL vs NoSQL, sharding strategy

anchors.1: Skipped requirements, jumped straight to architecture
anchors.2: Asked 1-2 questions but missed read:write ratio or scale
anchors.3: Covered functional + 2+ non-functional requirements, asked about scale
anchors.4: Proactively identified read-heavy workload, edge cases (duplicate URLs, custom alias conflicts), and clarified ambiguities

anchors.1: Single monolithic service with no caching or scaling considerations
anchors.2: Basic client-server with database, but no caching or separated read/write
anchors.3: Separated read/write services, cache layer, reasonable database choice
anchors.4: Clean separation of concerns, justified component choices, addressed failure modes

checklist: Explained key generation approach with collision handling

anchors.1: No depth on any component, hand-waved implementation details
anchors.2: Shallow discussion of 1 component, no trade-off analysis
anchors.3: Deep dive into 2+ components with concrete approaches and trade-offs
anchors.4: Thorough analysis with quantitative reasoning, failure modes, and alternative approaches compared

checklist: Cache hit ratio vs memory cost

anchors.1: Could not identify any trade-offs in the design
anchors.3: Discussed 2-3 trade-offs with pros/cons and justified choices
anchors.4: Systematically evaluated trade-offs across multiple dimensions with data-driven reasoning

commonMistakes: Not separating read and write paths despite 100:1 ratio
Using MD5/SHA hash without addressing collision handling
Forgetting to discuss 301 vs 302 redirect implications for analytics
Not considering how to handle custom alias conflicts
Ignoring cache invalidation on URL expiration
Not addressing hot key problem (viral URLs)
